fields.title: The Origins of Humanity
fields.content: Kenya is home to some of the oldest human fossils ever discovered. The Koobi Fora site near Lake Turkana has yielded fossils dating back over 3 million years, earning Kenya the title "Cradle of Humankind." The famous "Turkana Boy" fossil, found in 1984, is one of the most complete early human skeletons ever discovered.

fields.title: The Swahili Coast Civilization
fields.content: From the 8th to 16th centuries, the Kenyan coast was home to thriving Swahili city-states like Mombasa, Malindi, and Lamu. These cities were major trading hubs connecting Africa with Arabia, India, and China, trading gold, ivory, and spices for silk and porcelain.

fields.title: Fort Jesus - A UNESCO World Heritage Site
fields.content: Built by the Portuguese in 1593-1596, Fort Jesus in Mombasa is one of the most outstanding examples of 16th century military architecture. It changed hands nine times between the Portuguese and Arabs before being captured by the British in 1895. Today, it is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and museum.

fields.title: The Uganda Railway and the Birth of Nairobi
fields.content: The construction of the Uganda Railway between 1896 and 1901 transformed Kenya. The railway was built by Indian laborers, over 2,500 of whom died during construction. The railway depot at Mile 327 became the city of Nairobi, now Kenya's capital and the economic hub of East Africa.

fields.title: The Mau Mau Uprising
fields.content: The Mau Mau rebellion (1952-1960) was a pivotal armed struggle against British colonial rule. The Kikuyu-led uprising demanded land freedom and independence. Although brutally suppressed, the rebellion accelerated Kenya's path to independence and is now recognized as a heroic struggle for freedom.

fields.title: Jomo Kenyatta - The First President
fields.content: Jomo Kenyatta (c. 1897-1978) was Kenya's first President and a dominant figure in the independence struggle. He served as the first Prime Minister (1963-1964) and then President (1964-1978). His philosophy of "Harambee" (pulling together) became the national motto.

fields.title: The Great Rift Valley
fields.content: The Great Rift Valley runs through Kenya from north to south, stretching over 6,000 kilometers from Syria to Mozambique. In Kenya, it is home to stunning lakes (Naivasha, Nakuru, Bogoria), volcanoes, and is a UNESCO World Heritage site for its paleontological significance.

fields.title: Wangari Maathai - Nobel Peace Prize Winner
fields.content: Professor Wangari Maathai (1940-2011) was the founder of the Green Belt Movement, which planted over 51 million trees in Kenya. In 2004, she became the first African woman to win the Nobel Peace Prize for her contribution to sustainable development, democracy, and peace.

fields.title: The Kenyan Safari Experience
fields.content: Kenya is home to the "Big Five" (lion, leopard, elephant, rhino, buffalo) and the world-famous Great Migration, where over 1.5 million wildebeest and zebra cross the Mara River from Tanzania to Kenya. The Maasai Mara National Reserve is one of the most celebrated wildlife destinations on Earth.

fields.title: The 2010 Constitution - A New Dawn
fields.content: The Constitution of Kenya 2010 was a landmark document that replaced the 1963 independence constitution. It introduced devolution, a Bill of Rights, an independent judiciary, and reduced presidential powers. It was approved by 67% of voters in a 2010 referendum.

fields.question: Can police search my phone without a warrant?
fields.answer: Under Article 31 of the Kenyan Constitution, you have the right to privacy. Police generally need a warrant to search your phone. Exceptions: if you consent, or if you are arrested and the phone is evidence of that crime.

fields.question: What are my rights if I am arrested?
fields.answer: Under Article 49, you have the right to: be informed why you are arrested, remain silent, communicate with a lawyer, and be brought to court within 24 hours.

fields.question: Can police detain me without charge?
fields.answer: No. Under Article 49(1)(f), you must be brought to court within 24 hours of arrest. If not charged within that time, you must be released.

fields.question: Can police enter my home without a warrant?
fields.answer: Generally no. Under Article 31, police need a warrant to enter and search your home. Exceptions include emergencies or if you invite them in.

fields.question: Can the government take my land?
fields.answer: Under Article 40, the government can only take land for public purposes (roads, schools, hospitals) and must pay you prompt and full compensation.

fields.question: My landlord wants to evict me. What are my rights?
fields.answer: Your landlord must give you proper notice (usually 30 days for monthly tenancy) and cannot evict you without a court order. You can challenge illegal eviction in court.

fields.question: Can a woman inherit land?
fields.answer: Yes. The Constitution guarantees equality before the law. The Matrimonial Property Act and the Law of Succession Act protect women's rights to inherit and own property.

fields.question: Can my employer fire me without notice?
fields.answer: Under Article 41 and the Employment Act, you are entitled to notice or pay in lieu of notice. The notice period depends on how long you have worked.

fields.question: Do I have the right to join a trade union?
fields.answer: Yes. Article 41 guarantees your right to form, join, and participate in trade union activities.

fields.question: What is the minimum wage in Kenya?
fields.answer: The minimum wage varies by sector. As of 2024, the minimum wage for general labourers is approximately KES 15,000 per month. Check the Labour Institutions Act for specific rates.

fields.question: Do I have a right to healthcare?
fields.answer: Yes. Article 43 guarantees the right to the highest attainable standard of health, including healthcare services.

fields.question: Can a hospital refuse to treat me if I cannot pay?
fields.answer: In emergencies, no. Hospitals have a duty to provide emergency care regardless of ability to pay. For non-emergencies, they may require payment.

fields.question: Is education a constitutional right?
fields.answer: Yes. Article 43(1)(f) guarantees the right to education. The government has an obligation to provide basic education.

fields.question: Can a child be denied admission for lack of fees?
fields.answer: Under the Basic Education Act, no child can be denied admission based on inability to pay fees. Schools must provide education regardless.

fields.question: What is the legal age of marriage in Kenya?
fields.answer: The legal age of marriage is 18 years for both men and women. Child marriage is illegal under the Marriage Act and the Children Act.

fields.question: Can I get a divorce without going to court?
fields.answer: No. Divorce must be granted by a court. However, you can seek mediation or separation agreements as alternatives.

fields.question: How do I check if I am registered to vote?
fields.answer: You can check your voter registration status through the IEBC portal, by SMS to 20000, or by visiting your local IEBC office.

fields.question: What documents do I need to register to vote?
fields.answer: You need your original National ID card (or passport), and proof of residence (like a utility bill or letter from your chief).

fields.question: Is my personal data protected by law?
fields.answer: Yes. The Data Protection Act, 2019 protects your personal data. Organizations must get your consent to collect and use your data.
